verses.commentary: Besides the previous famine in Abraham's time (12:10), another comes. Isaac faces the same test his father did, going to Gerar and Abimelek. The pattern repeats across generations.
God explicitly forbids Egypt. Stay in the land I tell you. The promise requires remaining in Canaan, even during hardship.
The Abrahamic covenant is renewed: I will be with you, bless you, give these lands, and confirm my oath. The promise passes intact to the second generation.
The threefold promise repeated: descendants like stars, all these lands, and through your offspring all nations blessed. Complete covenant transfer to Isaac.
The blessing continues because Abraham obeyed—kept requirements, commands, decrees, and instructions. Obedience in one generation brings blessing to the next.
Isaac obeys and stays. Unlike Abraham who fled to Egypt (12:10), Isaac remains where God placed him.
Isaac repeats Abraham's sin exactly—the sister-wife deception out of fear. Like father, like son. The same faithlessness strategy despite God's recent promises.
Abimelek sees Isaac caressing (tsachaq, צָחַק—same root as Isaac's name, meaning laughter) Rebekah. The affection betrays the lie.
The pagan king confronts the patriarch's deception. Why did you lie? Isaac's excuse: fear of death. Faith fails under pressure.
Abimelek's rebuke echoes God's questions to Adam and Cain (3:13, 4:10). You could have brought guilt upon us. The pagan shows more righteousness than the promise-bearer.
Abimelek protects Isaac despite the deception, issuing a death threat to anyone who harms them. Grace from an unexpected source.
God blesses Isaac's farming with hundredfold yields despite his recent faithlessness. Divine blessing doesn't depend on perfect human performance.
Progressive prosperity: became rich, continued growing, became very wealthy. God's promise to bless materializes tangibly.
Massive flocks, herds, and servants create envy (qana, קָנָא) among the Philistines. Success breeds hostility.
Filling wells with dirt is economic warfare in arid lands. Destroying water access is destroying life and livelihood.
Forced departure. You've become too powerful for us. Success makes Isaac threatening. Blessing leads to expulsion.
Isaac responds peacefully, moving to the Valley of Gerar. No armed resistance, just obedient relocation.
Isaac reopens Abraham's wells that Philistines destroyed, restoring them with their original names. Reclaiming heritage and honoring father's legacy.
Fresh discovery: a well of living water (mayim chayim, מַיִם חַיִּים). Life-giving provision.
Conflict erupts. Esek (עֵשֶׂק) means contention or dispute. The name memorializes the conflict over resources.
Second well, same result. Sitnah (שִׂטְנָה) means opposition or hostility, sharing a root with Satan. Ongoing harassment.
Third well succeeds. Rehoboth (רְחֹבוֹת) means room or broad places. Now the LORD has given us room. Peace after persistence. God provides space for His people.
Isaac moves on to Beersheba, Abraham's old territory. Returning to covenant ground.

That very night God appears, reaffirming the covenant: I am the God of Abraham, I will bless you and increase your descendants. Divine reassurance after conflict. God speaks Abraham's name over Isaac.
Isaac builds an altar, calls on the LORD's name, and pitches his tent. Worship, proclamation, and settlement. Faith expressed publicly.
Abimelek comes seeking peace, bringing his advisor and army commander. Former enemies initiate reconciliation.
Isaac's pointed question: Why come now when you were hostile? Honest confrontation. Past wounds need acknowledgment.
Their answer: We saw clearly the LORD is with you. Even pagans recognize divine blessing. They want a treaty because God backs Isaac.
Let there be a sworn agreement. We didn't harm you, and we sent you away peacefully. They reframe history favorably, but the initiative for peace is genuine.
Isaac makes a feast. Covenant meals seal agreements. Breaking bread together signals new relationship.
Early morning oaths, then peaceful departure. Enemies become treaty partners. The name Beersheba means well of the oath—Abraham's treaty (21:31) now renewed with Isaac.
That same day, Isaac's servants report success: We've found water! Divine blessing punctuates the treaty. God provides.
He names it Shibah (oath), hence Beersheba. The name carries double meaning: well of the oath and well of seven. Commemorating both Abraham's and Isaac's covenants.
Esau at forty marries two Hittite women. Marrying outside the covenant family, like Ishmael (28:9). These marriages grieve his parents (v.35).
They were a source of grief (morat ruach, מֹרַת רוּחַ, bitterness of spirit) to Isaac and Rebekah. Esau's choices bring family pain. Sets up the blessing crisis in chapter 27.
